summary: In this talk, Yann LeCun discusses the history, power, and limits of deep learning and convolutional neural networks. He argues that while supervised and reinforcement learning have driven recent AI successes, true human-level intelligence requires self-supervised predictive learning to build robust models of the world, handle uncertainty, and enable reasoning and planning.

principles.statement: The success of neural networks stems from their ability to reflect the compositional nature of the world.
principles.rationale: The physical world is built hierarchically—objects are made of parts, which are made of motifs, which are made of edges. Architectures like convolutional networks succeed because they inherently model this compositional hierarchy.
principles.source_quote: what makes commercial Nets so successful in my opinion is that they reflect the fact that the world is compositional

principles.statement: Pure reinforcement learning is fundamentally inefficient and unsafe for real-world application.
principles.rationale: Reinforcement learning requires an enormous number of trials to achieve competence. In the physical world, you cannot run simulations faster than real-time, and trial-and-error can lead to fatal mistakes.
principles.source_quote: we can really use pure reinforcement learning in the real world because you know any action we take you kill you and also you can't win the real world fest every all time

principles.statement: Predictive learning is the mechanism by which intelligent agents develop representations of the world.
principles.rationale: By constantly trying to predict future states from current observations, a system naturally learns underlying physical concepts like depth and object permanence without needing explicit labels.
principles.source_quote: if your brain tries to predict what the world is going to look like when you have had the notion of death will naturally emerge as a representation of the world because that's the simplest explanation for the world changes

principles.statement: Reasoning is fundamentally a process of optimization.
principles.rationale: Whether it is probabilistic inference, planning a sequence of actions, or minimizing free energy, reasoning involves finding the optimal value of a variable by minimizing an objective function.
principles.source_quote: I think every form of reasoning is some form of optimization.

frameworks.name: Differentiable Programming
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building dynamic, complex AI systems that require flexible architectures (like memory networks or graph transformers) rather than fixed neural network structures.
steps: Define the architecture of the network dynamically using a program.
Run the program and keep track of all the operations that are being performed.
Automatically compute the gradient by backpropagating through the dynamically generated computational graph.
Update the parameters of the assembled modules to train them to do what you want.
frameworks.source_quote: you write a program based and given assembly modules you can train the properties of those modules to do what you want

frameworks.name: Adversarial Training for Video Prediction
frameworks.when_to_apply: When training a model to predict future states in a non-deterministic world where multiple futures are possible, to avoid blurry predictions.
steps: Build a predictor (generator) that looks at past frames and a source of random vectors to predict future frames.
Build a discriminator (energy function) that outputs a low value for real future events and a high value for fake/generated events.
Train the generator to produce outputs that the discriminator cannot distinguish from real events, bringing the predictions closer to the manifold of plausible futures.
frameworks.source_quote: the generator trench itself to produce outputs that is given either can sell are fake and catchy because it knows the gradients of the outputs of the this container will respectfully put that so much

mental_models.name: The Learning Cake
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the amount of information provided to a machine by different learning paradigms. Reinforcement learning provides very little information (the cherry), supervised learning provides some information via labels (the icing), and self-supervised learning provides massive amounts of information by predicting the input from the input (the bulk of the cake).
mental_models.example: Explaining why machines currently require 83 hours to learn an Atari game that a human learns in minutes: machines rely on the 'cherry' (RL) rather than the 'cake' (self-supervised predictive models of the world).

mental_models.name: The Data Manifold (Energy-Based Model)
mental_models.description: Viewing the real world as a low-dimensional manifold in a high-dimensional space of all possible states. An energy function is used to assign low energy to points on the manifold (plausible real-world states) and high energy to points outside it (impossible or fake states).
mental_models.example: When predicting a pen falling on a table, the exact direction is uncertain. Instead of punishing the model for guessing the wrong specific direction, the energy function only punishes the model if it predicts an outcome that defies physics (off the manifold).

heuristics: The more layers you can add, the kind of better it works if you do this carefully.
Computing the gradient takes just as much computation as computing the objective function.

signature_quotes.text: what I think is surprising is the power of gradient descent that greater design actually works if you have very large systems with many parameters
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the initial skepticism in the machine learning community regarding the effectiveness of backpropagation on large neural networks.

signature_quotes.text: what makes commercial Nets so successful in my opinion is that they reflect the fact that the world is compositional
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why Convolutional Neural Networks (transcribed as 'commercial Nets') are so effective at perception tasks.

signature_quotes.text: I think every form of reasoning is some form of optimization.
signature_quotes.context: Answering a question about how to integrate reasoning and common sense into deep learning systems.

anti_patterns.description: Relying purely on reinforcement learning for real-world autonomous systems.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It requires an enormous number of interactions to learn basic tasks, and in the real world, trial-and-error can be fatal or impossible to simulate faster than real-time.

anti_patterns.description: Using traditional supervised learning (like L2 loss) to predict future video frames.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Because the real world is unpredictable (e.g., a falling pen can land in many directions), the model will output the average of all possible futures, resulting in blurry, fuzzy predictions.
